title: Today's AI Brief: Rehumanizing global health care with agentic AI
summary: A look at AI's role in global healthcare and biodefense
imageAlt: Image from source article for Rehumanizing global health care with agentic AI
imageCaption: Rehumanizing global health care with agentic AI
imageSourceTitle: Rehumanizing global health care with agentic AI

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's AI Brief. I'm looking at some interesting developments in the world of artificial intelligence, particularly in global healthcare and biodefense. Let's dive in.

First up, the global healthcare sector is under strain. Decades of underinvestment and recruitment constraints have coincided with a surge in demand for services for aging populations. Now, agentic AI is being explored as a way to rehumanize global healthcare. This approach focuses on augmenting human capabilities, rather than replacing them. My read is that this could be a game-changer for healthcare professionals who are burnt out or struggling to keep up with demand.

Next, OpenAI has published an action plan for AI-powered biological resilience, dubbed 'Biodefense in the Intelligence Age'. This plan aims to harness AI's potential to improve our ability to detect, respond to, and prevent biological threats. It's an interesting move, and I think it's a great example of how AI can be used for good. The idea of using AI to enhance our defenses against biological threats is both practical and reassuring.

I'm also intrigued by the economics of AI models. The cost of training and deploying AI models is decreasing, making them more accessible to a wider range of organizations. This could lead to more innovative applications of AI in healthcare and other industries. For instance, smaller hospitals or clinics might be able to leverage AI to improve patient care, which would be a fantastic outcome.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
